For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Level 3 objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 10:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
print("\nAdjacency list:", seg['adjacency_list'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Level 3 has a different layout. Let me analyze the objects:
- id=0: color=b (blue), pixels=2718 - this is the main background
- id=1: color=Y (yellow), pixels=64 - right edge vertical bar (timer?)
- id=2: color=Y (yellow), pixels=63 - a shape at rows 3-14, cols 33-44
- id=3: color=Y (yellow), pixels=54 - a shape at rows 9-14, cols 12-20
- id=4: color=B (black), pixels=56 - character at rows 21-32, cols 12-23
- id=7: color=B (black), pixels=48 - P-shape at rows 27-32, cols 45-56
- id=19: color=Y (yellow), pixels=54 - a shape at rows 42-47, cols 9-20
- id=20: color=Y (yellow), pixels=63 - a shape at rows 42-53, cols 33-44
- id=21: color=S (sky blue), pixels=88 - horizontal bar at rows 48-50, cols 0-32
- id=22: color=S (sky blue), pixels=72 - horizontal bar at rows 48-50, cols 36-62
- id=43: color=b (blue), pixels=720 - another background region
- id=44: color=B (black), pixels=63 - bottom edge bar
So the layout has:
- Character (B) at rows 21-32, cols 12-23 (left side)
- P-shape (B) at rows 27-32, cols 45-56 (right side)
- Two Y-shapes: one at rows 9-14, cols 12-20 (top-left) and one at rows 3-14, cols 33-44 (top-right)
- Two more Y-shapes: one at rows 42-47, cols 9-20 (bottom-left) and one at rows 42-53, cols 33-44 (bottom-right)
- Two S bars: horizontal at rows 48-50
Wait, let me look at the image more carefully. The image shows:
- Top-left: a yellow L-shape
- Top-right: a yellow F-shape
- Middle-left: a black L-shape (character?)
- Middle-right: a black shape (P-shape?)
- Bottom-left: a yellow shape
- Bottom-right: a yellow shape
- A horizontal white/light blue bar near the bottom
Actually, looking at the image, there's a horizontal divider (S color) at rows 48-50. Above it, there are shapes. Below it, there's a blue region.
Let me think about the goal. In Level 1 and 2, the goal was to move the character so that the P-shape overlaps a Y-shape. In Level 3, there are multiple Y-shapes. Maybe the goal is to overlap the P-shape with one of them.
But wait, the layout is different. The character and P-shape are both B (black) color. In Level 2, the character was B and the P-shape was c (charcoal). Let me check if the colors are different.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, both id=4 and id=7 have color=B. So they're the same color. But they might be different objects.
Let me check the shapes:
- id=4 (character): boundary=[[21, 12], [21, 14], [29, 14], [30, 15], [30, 23], [32, 23], [32, 12]] - this is an L-shape
- id=7 (P-shape): boundary=[[27, 45], [27, 56], [29, 56], [29, 54], [30, 53], [32, 53], [32, 48], [30, 48], [29, 47], [29, 45]] - this is a more complex shape
The Y-shapes:
- id=3: boundary=[[9, 12], [9, 17], [11, 17], [12, 18], [12, 20], [14, 20], [14, 9], [12, 9], [12, 11], [11, 12]] - L-shape at top-left
- id=2: boundary=[[3, 33], [3, 44], [5, 44], [5, 36], [6, 35], [14, 35], [14, 33]] - F-shape at top-right
- id=19: boundary=[[42, 9], [42, 20], [44, 20], [44, 18], [45, 17], [47, 17], [47, 12], [45, 12], [44, 11], [44, 9]] - L-shape at bottom-left
- id=20: boundary=[[42, 33], [42, 35], [50, 35], [51, 36], [51, 44], [53, 44], [53, 33]] - F-shape at bottom-right
So there are two types of Y-shapes: L-shapes (id=3, id=19) and F-shapes (id=2, id=20).
The character (id=4) is an L-shape, and the P-shape (id=7) is a more complex shape. Let me check if the P-shape matches any of the Y-shapes.
Actually, let me compare the hashes to see which shapes are the same.
Let me try to understand the control scheme. In Level 2, the character was on the right and the P-shape was on the left. In Level 3, the character is on the left and the P-shape is on the right. This is similar to Level 1.
Let me try LEFT to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare hashes to identify matching shapes
seg = current_frame.segmentation
shapes = {}
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 30:
        h = n['hash']
        if h not in shapes:
            shapes[h] = []
        shapes[h].append((n['id'], n['color'], n['pixels']))

print("Shape groups by hash:")
for h, items in shapes.items():
    print(f"  hash={h}: {items}")

# Try LEFT to see what moves
action(['LEFT'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter LEFT:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('B', 'c') and n['pixels'] > 30:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
